terms.definition: A battery-operated standalone electronic machine used in Indian elections to record votes. It has two units: the Ballot Unit (which voter uses) and the Control Unit (which the polling officer controls). EVMs have been used since 1982 and became universal in 2004.
terms.example: When you press the button next to your candidate's name on the EVM, a beep confirms your vote is recorded.

terms.definition: A device attached to the EVM that prints a paper slip showing the candidate's name, number and party symbol for 7 seconds after you vote. The slip then drops into a sealed box. It allows voters to verify their vote was recorded correctly.
terms.example: After pressing the button on the EVM, Priya saw a paper slip with 'Congress — Hand Symbol' appear for 7 seconds, confirming her vote.

terms.definition: An option on the ballot/EVM that lets voters reject all candidates on the list. Introduced in 2013 by the Supreme Court. NOTA votes are counted but a candidate with even 1 vote will win if NOTA gets the most 'votes'.
terms.example: If none of the 5 candidates in your constituency seem capable, you can press NOTA to express your dissatisfaction.

terms.definition: A set of guidelines issued by the Election Commission of India that comes into effect when elections are announced. It prevents the ruling party from using government resources, making new policy announcements or distributing freebies to influence voters.
terms.example: After MCC kicked in, the state government could not launch new welfare schemes until results were declared.

terms.definition: An autonomous constitutional body established under Article 324 of the Indian Constitution. It is responsible for administering election processes for the Parliament, State Legislatures, and offices of President and Vice-President of India.
terms.example: ECI announced a 7-phase schedule for the 2024 Lok Sabha elections.

terms.definition: A geographical division of the country for electoral purposes. Each constituency elects one representative. India has 543 Lok Sabha constituencies and 4,033 state assembly constituencies.
terms.example: Mumbai South is a Lok Sabha constituency that elects one Member of Parliament.

terms.definition: A government officer (usually a District Collector or similar senior officer) appointed by the ECI to oversee the election process in a constituency. They accept nominations, supervise polling and declare results.
terms.example: The Returning Officer rejected a candidate's nomination because his affidavit had incorrect information.

terms.definition: A sworn legal document that every election candidate must file along with their nomination papers, disclosing their personal details, criminal cases (if any), assets, liabilities, and educational qualifications. This information is public.
terms.example: Voters can check a candidate's affidavit on the ECI website to see their criminal record and wealth.

terms.definition: The official Voter ID card issued by the Election Commission. It serves as proof that you are registered on the electoral roll and as a photo ID. You must show this or one of 11 approved alternative documents to vote.
terms.example: Ramesh showed his EPIC card at the polling booth to receive his voting slip.

terms.definition: The official list of all eligible voters in a constituency. Also called the 'voter list'. Updated annually. You must be on the electoral roll to vote. Check your name at voters.eci.gov.in.
terms.example: Ananya found her name on the electoral roll of Pune's Kasba constituency.

terms.definition: The formal process by which a person declares their candidacy for an election. They must submit nomination papers, a security deposit (₹25,000 for LS, ₹10,000 for VS, half for SC/ST), and an affidavit to the Returning Officer.
terms.example: 25 candidates filed nominations for the Mumbai North constituency.

terms.definition: A survey of voters conducted immediately after they leave the polling booth, asking how they voted. Results are published only after all phases of voting are complete. Not 100% accurate.
terms.example: Exit polls predicted NDA would win 350 seats, but the actual result was 293.

terms.definition: A survey conducted before elections asking people who they plan to vote for. These are published during the campaign period. ECI restricts exit polls and opinion polls 48 hours before polling.

An election held in a single constituency between general elections, when the sitting MP or MLA dies, resigns, is disqualified or their election is set aside by court. Also called 'Upchunav' in Hindi.
terms.example: After the MP from Rae Bareli resigned to contest Lok Sabha from another seat, a by-election was held.

terms.definition: A situation where no single party or pre-poll alliance wins a majority (272+ seats in Lok Sabha). The President then invites the single largest party or a coalition to form a government and prove majority on the floor of the house.
terms.example: The 2014 election resulted in a hung parliament in Delhi, leading to President's Rule.

terms.definition: An alliance of multiple political parties that come together to form a government when no single party has a majority. Partners share cabinet portfolios and agree on a Common Minimum Programme.
terms.example: The NDA is a coalition led by BJP with smaller parties like JD(U) and TDP.

terms.definition: The electoral system used in India and the UK. The candidate who gets the most votes in a constituency wins — even without a majority. Simple but can mean a party wins with only 30% of votes.
terms.example: In a 4-way contest, a candidate who gets 30% of the vote can win while 70% voted against them.

terms.definition: An electoral system where parties get seats in proportion to the total votes they receive nationally. Used in the EU Parliament and many European countries. More representative but can lead to fragmented parliaments.

The system used in the United States where voters technically elect 'Electors' (538 total) who then formally elect the President. A candidate needs 270 electoral votes to win. Each state's electoral votes are roughly proportional to its population.
terms.example: In 2000, Al Gore won the popular vote but George W. Bush won the Electoral College and became President.

terms.definition: An official of a political party in Parliament whose job is to ensure party MPs attend sessions and vote according to the party's decisions. 'Three-line whip' is the strictest, requiring mandatory attendance and voting. Breaking a whip can lead to disqualification under anti-defection law.

The 10th Schedule of the Indian Constitution (added 1985) which disqualifies an MP or MLA who votes against the party's directive or voluntarily gives up party membership. Prevents 'aaya ram gaya ram' political switching.
The official timetable issued by ECI announcing all key dates: announcement, nomination, withdrawal, polling and counting. Once announced, the MCC comes into effect immediately.
The 48-hour period before polling starts when all election campaigning must stop. Political speeches, rallies, ads and canvassing are prohibited. Also called 'campaign silence'.
An electoral malpractice where one party forcibly takes over a polling booth and casts votes illegally. It is a cognizable offence under Section 135A of the Representation of the People Act 1951.

The designated physical location where eligible voters go to cast their votes on election day. Each polling station serves a specific area from the electoral roll. Booths must have drinking water, a ramp for disabled, separate queues for men and women.
A system where a registered voter can authorise another person to vote on their behalf. In India, only service voters (military personnel posted away from home) are allowed proxy voting.

A mechanism that allows certain voters to vote by post without visiting a polling booth. Eligible categories in India include: government servants on duty, military personnel, senior citizens (80+), disabled voters, and COVID-affected voters.

The government official in charge of a specific polling station on election day. Responsible for ensuring orderly conduct, operating the EVM, verifying voter identity and maintaining the station diary.
